speaker: interviewer
text: --Wolf. Rabbi Wolf, you were telling me, you were arriving to New York and you saw the Statue of Liberty. What did know about that statue at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, but having been to Paris, I had learned there that the Statue of Liberty was a gift of the French people to the United States of America. But that had relatively little impact on the feelings that I had as the ship pulled into the harbor of New York. In fact, compared to the-- to being in the harbor of New York, even the Statue of Liberty was a relatively small matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody wait for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had relatives on my mother's side, the members of the rather extensive David family, who met me and in whose home I spent the three days that we were scheduled to stay in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the rest of them stay? The rest of the students.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that most of them had either relatives or acquaintances in New York. I don't recall any of them staying in a hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were staying in New York, did you meet each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm quite sure that on one or two occasions we went to see some sight in New York together. But most of the time, as far as I recall, we were on our own. And the-- my hosts were able to show me the ropes of get around in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your impression, first impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a big place. And the skyscrapers were real. They weren't just pictures. Frankly, I remember very little specifics about my-- that-- those three days in New York. One thing that I became aware of was the-- how radical the difference in pronunciation was, of-- even though everybody spoke the same language. I-- for instance, I recall my hostess, who was a first cousin of my mother's, calling her one son who was playing out in the streets, 'Boinie,' which of course I would have spelled B-O-I-N-I-E. And then I found out that it really was B-E-R-N-I-E, and I was staying in the Bronx. Another impression, of course, was the New York subway, which was so much more extensive than the other-- only other subway that I'd experienced, in Berlin. And I remember it specifically from being told how to get to Brooklyn from the Bronx by subway, because a family from Eberbach lived in the-- in Brooklyn, and they invited me to have lunch with them. Now, they were not Jewish, and they had arranged for a kosher lunch at their home. And I successfully transacted my trip on the-- on the subway, changing once or twice, and got to Brooklyn, found my way to their home. I was very proud of that accomplishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember this family's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Their name was Kadel. K-A-D-E-L. And Mrs. Kadel was a-- nee Wieprecht. W-I-E-P-R-E-C-H-T, which was her family in-- in Eberbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, why did so many Germans come to the United States? Opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family in New York. Were they curious about what's going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were fairly well informed by correspondence and by reading the papers. And yes, they asked for specifics of what was happening in Eberbach.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they think the future--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they knew it was a-- a terrible mess. It was something to worry about, and certainly understood my coming. And they were trying to bring over any of their immediate family, and successfully did so.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave New York, and where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in New York three days, spent one day in Washington to sightsee in Washington, which we did all five of us together, and then took a Baltimore and Ohio train from-- an overnight train from Washington to Cincinnati. Was-- had all the-- the problems of an overnight coach trip. We were not-- we did not travel by sleeper, and one of the things that impressed us was that to ride in that coach when in the early morning hours a man stood up and put on his tallit and tfillin, and said his morning prayers. That's something we had never seen in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrived in Cincinnati, where we were met by Dr. Nelson Glueck, who then was an assistant professor at the Hebrew Union College, and who was given the chore of meeting the students, because he happened to own a station wagon. The-- at any rate, we got off the train in-- in Cincinnati, thought that somebody might meet us direct-- right at the train. But nobody seemed to be looking for us, so we were among the last to go from the train into the station. And a fairly young looking man very reluctantly approached us and in fairly good German asked [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And the reason he was so hesitant-- Translate. Translate that.

speaker: interviewee
text: --is that, we found out later, he had gone to another group that had come into the station, and they looked at him. And he then repeated the question in English, and they said, no, we are such and such a band from Brooklyn. The reason he was doubly hesitant to approach us is that one of the five of us was carrying his precious cello, so he-- he was afraid of musicians. At any rate, he introduced himself as-- in good German-- as Dr. Nelson Gluek and packed us into his station wagon, and took us to-- to his home for breakfast. During that breakfast, something interesting happened. The-- a butler carried a tray. We didn't know what was on the tray, except that the-- as-- as he came through the door, Dr. Gluek mentioned to his mother-in-law, in whose home we really were meeting, and she sent the butler back out. It took a little while before butler came back with five platters of scrambled eggs. What we found out later was the reason for Dr. Gluek sending the first load back to the kitchen is that they was serving the eggs with bacon. So since we did not know that at that time, this was one introduction to American Reformed Judaism that we almost got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the first day you went into the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- we were impressed by the size of the school. I've got to tell you the Hebrew Union College in Cincinnati, which was built in its present-- or in its then shape-- in the early 1900's, except for its library, which was more recent, was a-- an extensive campus with three or four imposing buildings, far beyond anything that we had seen owned by any Jewish organization in Germany. So that by itself impressed us. The-- the dormitory, where each of us had his own room, again was something that was most impressive to us, because we were not aware of any facility of that sort, even in a secular school in Germany. The-- as soon as-- after breakfast we arrived at the school, we-- we-- we were welcomed by a number of-- of students. The school year hadn't started yet, so the-- the whole student body wasn't there yet. And every one of the students did his best-- and I can say his, because there were no-- no girl students at that time-- they did his best to make us feel welcome and at home. And another aspect of the welcome was that they all were very, very curious of-- about our experience in Nazi Germany. The most important element of the day was our being invited to the office of Dr. Morgenstern, the president of the school, who formally welcomed us, told us that the entire community was excited about the arrival of these five people, that he-- they want us to feel as welcome as-- as possible, even though, of course, he anticipated that we would have some difficulty with the language, that we had a lot to learn about the-- the way of-- of studying in America in-- and in English. He assured us that we-- as far as religious practices are concerned, that of course American Reform was more radical than what we were used to in Germany, but that if we felt more at home wearing a head covering, that we should feel free to do so, even though in American Reform that was not the custom. This caused us some problem later. Even though it was a very minor problem, I think it is well worth telling the story. Part of our experience was to be invited into many Jewish homes and Jewish institutions. So one Sabbath morning, we, the five of us, went to the Rockdale Avenue Temple in Cincinnati for the Sabbath morning service. By that time we knew to anticipate a service which was almost entirely in English. We also knew that Dr. Phillipson was the oldest surviving-- Dr. Phillipson, the-- who was the senior rabbi of that congregation, was the oldest surviving graduate of the first class of the Hebrew Union College. He had been ordained in 1881, or '82. At any rate, it was a Classical Reform congregation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So soon after that, Dr. Phillipson invited the five of us to have lunch with him, which was a very delightful and good lunch. And afterwards, after the lunch, he interrupted the flow of the congregate conversation by saying, I noticed you gentlemen in my service last Saturday, and I noticed that you were wearing head coverings. I want you to know that this is not the custom in American Reform Judaism, and I would appreciate your not doing that again. So while Dr. Morgenstern was more liberal, even though he too was a Classical Reformed Jew, there were many Reform Jews to whom any Orthodox custom was just something that was not allowed. How did that make you feel? How-- how did you take that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we were, of course, shocked by it, because we had been in-- in another synagogue where there was no problem. And around the Hebrew Union College we had had-- encountered no problem from any of the students or the faculty. It was just one of the many culture shocks that we had to endure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who financed your studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hebrew Union College. We-- we were given a full scholarship, including room and board, and an opportunity of doing some work, for which we were paid $0.50 an hour, which at that time was the accepted pay for student labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your contact with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your contact with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our regular contact was by airmail letter. No, no. I must change that. Our-- our normal contact was by letter that usually took a week, a week and a half, to cross the-- the ocean. We watched ship schedules to make sure that we would get a-- our letters in to New York at the time when a fast ship would leave that would cross the ocean in five or six days, rather than on a steamer that took six or even eight or nine days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the letters went very regularly, even though we had no immediate information on anything. From time to time, there was the chance of sending a-- a letter by Zeppelin. The Hindenburg hadn't crashed yet, and it and one other ship would cross maybe once a month or something like that, at which time with-- for extra-- with extra pay, we could send a-- a faster letter. How often did the Hindenburg cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about once a month, but I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever seen the Hindenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I saw one of its sister ships, which came over Eberbach, because one of the-- the-- one of Zeppelin's coworkers in-- had been from Eberbach, and so the-- the ship came, flew over the city, and then went its way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the news from overseas? Of course, the hard news we got-- had to get from the paper, because the-- all-- all letters were-- were examined. The-- the letters arrived, having been opened by the-- by the authorities, and resealed. So all they could write was personal things, and about the-- those events happened to them that had nothing to do with the government. You-- how long did you stay in Cincinnati?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: In between, you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I came to Cincinnati in 1935, spent two school years, '35-'36, '36-'37, during which time I not only attended the Hebrew Union College but also the University of Cincinnati. And I graduated with a BA in 1937, but couldn't go to the graduation, because by that time I was on the ship to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I ask you, why did you go to college also?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was required. In order to get a rabbinic degree, you had to have a BA.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you go back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Germany because I was in the states on a student visa, which did not make permanent residence possible. In order to become a permanent resident, which I had decided to do, I needed a-- an immigration visa. And that, at that time, could be obtained only in one's country of origin. So my parents went to the police to find out whether there was any reason to believe that upon re-entering the country I would get into trouble. And they were sure that I would not. Then, and only then, did I book my trip to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you return? What transportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again on the Cunard line, this time on-- I can't think of the name of the steamer now. It was the last four stacked steamer. And it was a beautiful crossing, the-- the-- all the way, no ripple in the ocean, unlike the first trip. And I had a-- a wonderful time on the-- on the ship. I stopped in England first, to spend a week in London. Then stopped in Holland to visit with members of the family. And tried, while I was in Holland, to get my visa at the American consulate in Rotterdam. But no, was against the rules. They couldn't do it. I had to go to Germany. So then I-- I traveled on from Holland to Germany and spent, I think, about five or six weeks there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you cross the border to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not sure. The-- there were quite a number of places. I do remember that when I left Germany, then we did a lot of investigation so that I took a train that crossed-- and, again, I don't remember the name of the-- of the town-- in a place where the quote 'old' unquote border police were still on duty, and not the new Nazi stormtrooper police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to returning into Germany. When you crossed the border, did you see any change? Did you feel any-- any difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately, no. There was-- I do not recall seeing anything immediately, even though I might. I don't recall it. What I do recall is-- is my general feeling of being in Germany in general, and being at home in particular. It felt like being interned, like living in an institution that was surrounded by an invisible wall. Because you could be a complete human being only when you were inside your-- your own home or the homes of other Jews, or possibly the few real good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any unpleasant experiences while you were back?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one really frightening experience I recall was that very soon after I arrived in Germany, and arrived with-- rather arrived at my parents' home, there was an order that all passports held by Jews had to be turned in to the authorities for some kooky purpose. And as much as I couldn't have possibly get a visa or leave the country without a passport, that with the most threatening thing that could have happened. And I lived through a couple of very scary weeks. And it was not until about two days or so after I had my appointment with the American consulate in Stuttgart that my passport was returned. But it was returned. Now, I assume that this was just a coincident, rather than just planned chicanery. But it could very well have been, because they couldn't have made-- made me feel much worse than having-- being in Nazi Germany without a passport to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Heidelberg while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think I got to Heidelberg once or twice while I was there. Why are you asking?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the seminary still active?

speaker: interviewee
text: The seminary was not in Heidelberg. The seminary was in Berlin. As far as I know, the seminary was still in existence. I believe it got-- went out of existence in 1938 or 1939, when that type of thing was more likely to happen after Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was different in '37 when you returned, in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: While in 1935, most people that I knew at all would still communicate, by 1937 there were very few people with whom I could or would communicate. That-- that is what caused the sense of incarceration without a wall. There was this wall of-- well, the-- the-- I think it is the [NON-ENGLISH] or the-- the-- some of the very fundamentalist and exclusive sects that have the custom of shunning a person who has been excommunicated. I learned what it meant to be shunned.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the same people, having gone-- having grown, I'd say, at least a decade older during the two years that I was gone, because they lived in constant-- under constant pressure. And with always new wrinkles in the kind of troubles that the government shaped for its-- for its Jewish inhabitants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe some of the problems they had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's-- that's extremely difficult, because being a-- a born optimist, my memory has a strong prejudice against bad memories. And I-- I have forgotten most of those details, while I have not forgotten the general feeling that I've just described. The-- really, nothing specific that was harmful happened. If somebody can be in a place for six weeks and the worst possible thing that can happen to him was to have his-- not to have his passport on him, you can draw your conclusion. Nothing dramatic happened in those six weeks. In a sense, they were very boring weeks, because nothing-- nothing bad happened. And there was no way for anything good to happen, because most places of interest, I wouldn't be able to go to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know whether your parents had been planning to leave the country at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they definitely were not planning at that time. I tried to convince them, but they said, no, we're-- we are still making sort of a living, and this nonsense is bound to-- to stop sometime, we hope soon. Remember, this was before Kristallnacht, and the-- I would say a very large percentage of German Jews did not realize the danger until then.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop again.
